one_line_takeaway: Using an LLM as a short-lived 'teacher' that guides an attention-based RL 'student' during the first 10% of training gives higher driving success rates and real-time (~4 ms) inference, avoiding the multi-second latency of running an LLM as the live decision-maker.
key_finding: TeLL-Drive attains the highest success rates in all three scenarios (88% intersection, 91% ramp merge, 100% four-lane cruise) while keeping per-decision inference time at ~0.003-0.004 s, roughly 1000x faster than the LLM-based Dilu baseline (3.9-6.2 s), which cannot meet real-time constraints. The ablation shows LLM guidance plus attention (LA-PPO) converges faster and to higher reward than attention-only or vanilla PPO, and the DRL Student ultimately surpasses the LLM Teacher's success rate in every scenario.
core_contribution: TeLL-Drive, a teacher-student framework for autonomous-driving decision-making that uses an LLM (GPT-4o-mini) as a 'Teacher' to guide an attention-based deep-RL 'Student' policy. The LLM Teacher comprises three modules: a Decision Engine (computes a Time-to-Conflict-Point risk metric via rotation-projection and retrieves the most similar historical scenario by cosine similarity to build a chain-of-thought prompt), a Memory Repository (stores state/action/consequence tuples, capacity 20), and a Reflective Evaluator (flags episodes whose max risk exceeds a threshold and asks the LLM to propose policy/prompt/constraint adjustments). The Student is an actor-critic agent whose policy network embeds a Transformer self-attention layer that distills and fuses the Teacher's implicit policy with its own exploration; a KL-divergence constraint (tolerance sigma, kept small early then relaxed) keeps the Student close to the Teacher during early training. The LLM acts as Teacher for only the first 10% of training steps, after which the agent explores independently, so LLM latency is not incurred at deployment.
threat_model: None. This is not a security paper: there is no adversary, attacker, or attack/defense. 'Safety' here means ordinary collision avoidance and traffic-rule compliance, not resistance to a malicious actor. The paper was clustered under 'Autonomous Vehicle Security' but contains no threat model, and none should be inferred.
limitations: The paper's own Table II does not fully support its stated claim of superior average return: Dilu achieves a higher Eval Reward than TeLL-Drive in the intersection (6.59 vs 5.68) and four-lane/highway (29.53 vs 27.17) scenarios, with TeLL-Drive clearly ahead on reward only in the merge scenario (5.60 vs 3.21); its consistent advantages are success rate and decision latency, not reward per se. Evaluation is confined to Highway-Env simulation plus a single-scenario vehicle-in-loop demo (only qualitative case studies, no quantitative real-vehicle metrics). Only GPT-4o-mini is tested as the Teacher; no sensitivity to LLM choice, and no reported number of random seeds/runs behind the success-rate percentages. The action space is coarse high-level maneuvers delegated to PID, so low-level control quality is out of scope. Memory Repository capacity is very small (20).

datasets_used: Highway-Env + OpenAI Gym simulation, three scenarios: unsignalized intersection (unprotected left turn), high-speed ramp merging, four-lane adaptive cruise
Vehicle-in-loop platform: real vehicle fused with TESSNG microscopic simulation at Tongji University Autonomous Driving Smart Town (intersection scenario)
quantitative_results: Success rate (Ours): intersection 88%, ramp merge 91%, four-lane/highway 100%
Decision/inference time: Ours ~0.003-0.004 s vs Dilu 3.906-6.166 s (traditional DRL ~0.002-0.003 s)
Intersection Eval Reward: Ours 5.68 vs Dilu 6.59, DQN 3.22, A2C 2.88, RecurrentPPO 0.86
Ramp merge: Ours 91% success / 5.60 reward; A2C 0% success (highest speed 28.28 m/s) / -5.20 reward; Dilu 46% / 3.21; RecurrentPPO 30%; DQN 83%
Four-lane/highway Eval Reward: Ours 27.17 (100% success) vs Dilu 29.53 (78%), RecurrentPPO 25.55 (88%), DQN 22.02 (71%), A2C 21.95 (50%)
Ours avg speed / delta-TTCP: intersection 7.36 m/s / 4.92 s; merge 16.50 m/s / 1.31 s; highway 23.53 m/s / 2.13 s
Hyperparameters: LLM Teacher active first 10% of >=1e5 training steps; lr 5e-4; gamma 0.99; batch 128; rollout buffer 1600; memory capacity 20; few-shot examples 3; two 128-dim attention heads
baselines_compared: Ablation: Vanilla-PPO (V-PPO), Attention-PPO (A-PPO), LLM-Guided Attention-PPO (LA-PPO, theirs)
